text: This is a full-length video of a robotic right recurrent inguinal scrotal hernia repair. The patient has had a prior laparoscopic TEP repair with three DMAX.
The surgeon utilizes four ports for the procedure. In the left lower quadrant, a ProGrasp instrument is placed, while the left hand holds a forced bipolar and the right hand holds scissors.

The video shows an incarcerated right inguinal scrotal hernia in the patient's right groin. The left side displays evidence of a prior three DMAX repair with clam shelling at the lower edge, but no recurrent hernia is visible.

The surgeon creates a peritoneal flap in the right inguinal scrotal region, aiming to access the retro rectus space or pre transverse salus fascia space. The inferior epigastric vessels are identified as a landmark, which may be scarred to the mesh or other structures.
The surgeon dissects in the retropubic space, navigating around the mesh placed during the previous laparoscopic TEP repair, which is typically located in the retrorectus space.

The surgeon identifies the upper edge of the three D max mesh located in the retrorectus space adjacent to the inferior epigastric vessels. The peritoneum is taken down to access the preperitoneal space, with the intention to stay pre-peritoneal during the redo inguinal hernia operation.

The surgeon carefully dissects along the peritoneum, aiming to separate it from the testicular vessels. This maneuver is particularly useful in inguinal scrotal hernia repairs and redo situations, where the mesh is often not deeply positioned.
The surgeon uses a third grasper or fourth arm to provide counter tension, allowing the left hand to assist in precise dissection within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses their left hand to provide counter tension while dissecting along the peritoneum, carefully pushing away the surrounding fat tissue.
The surgeon carefully dissects the hernia sac, noting that the cecum forms part of the sac wall, requiring meticulous handling to avoid damage.
The surgeon identifies an avascular plane while hugging the peritoneum, revealing the colon as the posterior wall of the sac.
The surgeon uses a third arm to assist in dissection near the mesh, carefully navigating around the visible inferior epigastric vessels and vas deferens.

The surgeon carefully navigates the surgical field, using a grasper or scissors to press on the tissue and identify subtle changes. The mesh is hugged closely while searching for the line of areolar tissue. The surgeon crosses the midline and encounters a large vessel, prompting a reassessment and a shift to easier tasks.

The surgeon identifies the bladder in the surgical field, having dissected lateral tissue in the space of Bogros behind Cooper's ligament. The focus shifts to the challenging area where the inferior epigastric vessels join the external iliac artery.

The surgeon identifies a hole in the sac and carefully maneuvers to hug the mesh. The lower edge of the mesh is visible, showing some curling. The surgeon reaches the posterior extent of the mesh, noting additional mesh present. The infrapigastric and external iliac vein are indicated to be on the opposite side of the mesh, necessitating caution as the surgeon approaches the posterior edge.

The surgical field shows a fatty area with no bladder present, while the bladder is visible to the left. The surgeon maintains a bloodless dissection to differentiate tissue types, noting that staining with blood makes it difficult to distinguish between fat and other tissues.
The surgeon carefully works around the internal ring, freeing the attachments of the sac. Progress is made in detaching these small attachments that prevent the sac from exiting the scrotum.

The surgeon carefully dissects between the sac and surrounding tissue, briefly identifying the vas deferens. The area contains fat, which is safely removed, while maintaining hemostasis to preserve the visible tissue plane between the bladder and fat.

The surgeon identifies the tinea of the right colon while attempting to reduce the hernia sac. The dissection continues posteriorly to free up attachments, with visible pulsations of the external iliac artery as the fat is pushed away from the hernia.

The assistant applies external pressure on the scrotum, causing some fat to be pushed out. The surgeon attempts to pull the freed-up sac from within the surgical field, repeating a maneuver previously attempted during the procedure.
The surgeon successfully extracts the appendix from the scrotum, revealing the entire incarcerated hernia sac. The hernia sac is now fully exposed, allowing for the completion of its dissection.

The surgeon carefully examines the tip of the hernia sac, identifying the vas deferens and ensuring it is not divided. The vas deferens is visible in the posterior area, confirming safety in that region. Anteriorly, the surgeon cautiously assesses the tissue, determining it is not the testicular vessels, and proceeds with more aggressive division.
The surgeon has completely reduced the hernia sac, ensuring hemostasis is maintained. The surgical field is assessed for any damage.
The surgeon manipulates the hernia sac and the right colon, aiming to achieve a seven to eight centimeter posterior mesh overlap. Lymphatic fluid is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon connects the space of Bogros to the retropubic space, ensuring the mesh transitions smoothly from medial to lateral. The areolar plane is visible between the fat adjacent to the femoral vessels and the fat near the bladder, facilitated by maintaining hemostasis.

The surgeon observes the mesh integration medially, indicating a low likelihood of medial recurrence. Dissection continues in the area, with careful consideration given to the extent of mesh coverage past the midline and into the retropubic space, especially in the presence of scar tissue.

The surgeon completes the dissection and evaluates the old mesh, noting it is curled and adhered to the epigastric vessels. The mesh appears loose in some areas, prompting the surgeon to attempt freeing and excising it. The mesh is identified as a 3D Max regular type.

The surgeon attempts to reduce the mesh burden in the patient's right groin by carefully removing the mesh. Attention is given to the posterior lateral area to avoid damaging the genital femoral nerve and the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve, with caution exercised if the mesh is firmly adhered.

The surgeon identifies the posterior lateral edge of the mesh, which appears mobile and easy to remove. They separate the mesh from the surrounding fatty tissue. In the lower area, there is significant scar tissue, and the mesh is well integrated near the femoral branch and genital femoral nerve. A small portion of mesh is left behind while the unattached piece is resected.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to divide the polypropylene mesh, staying close to the mesh surface. As the dissection progresses medially, the inferior epigastric vessels are encountered, revealing that they are tightly adhered to the mesh. The surgeon decides to leave the mesh portion medial to the inferior epigastrics intact and excise the lateral section.

The surgeon identifies a piece of fat, likely a core lipoma, adjacent to the mesh. The mesh is positioned on the exterior side of the lipoma, and the surgeon prepares to excise both the lipoma and approximately one-third of the mesh.

The surgeon observes the mesh placed superficially, noting larger veins within the surrounding fatty tissue. The mesh appears to have inadequate posterior overlap, potentially contributing to hernia recurrence.

The surgeon removes a fatty clump of tissue, revealing the well-incorporated mesh left in place. The surgical field is measured to determine the size of the new mesh needed, with the largest anatomical probe grip being sixteen by twelve centimeters.

The surgeon selects a large pro grip mesh, originally sized at twenty by fifteen centimeters, and trims it to nineteen by fifteen centimeters for optimal anterior-posterior coverage. The mesh is oriented with the longer side measuring nineteen centimeters to ensure broad coverage while avoiding suturing in the triangle of pain and triangle of doom.

This is a lighter weight mesh and so I do have some concerns about using a lighter weight mesh But I felt that this was the best mesh that I had with this situation. I would have preferred a medium weight mesh, but there is not a medium weight pro grip. And if I use a medium weight mesh, such as parietine or barbed soft mesh, I would not have a way of securing the mesh posteriorly. And that would run the risk of the mesh parachuting into the inguinal scrotal defect. And with the location of this hernia, this indirect hernia, I did not feel comfortable closing the defect as it would risk injuring the general femoral nerve.
The surgeon deploys the mesh by folding it like a taco, drawing two criss-crossing lines to identify the center of the indirect hernia defect.
The surgeon observes the lightweight mesh securely positioned, with a small portion protruding above the flap. The excess mesh is trimmed and removed using a piece of 3D Max mesh.
The surgeon cuts the large mesh into narrower strips, resembling an orange peel, to facilitate removal through the eight millimeter port.
The surgeon removes the mesh easily and proceeds to close the peritoneal flap using a 3-0 V-Lock suture, measuring 189 inches in length.

The surgeon identifies a sliding hernia posteriorly, noting the absence of some of the hernia sac. Upon reaching the colon, a visible rim of the sac is observed, prompting the surgeon to close the visible hole using sutures.

The surgeon uses the angiocath technique to manipulate the colon, aiming to keep it on one side of the peritoneum. After closing the hole, an additional V-Lock suture is prepared to close the remaining peritoneal defect.

The fellow inserts a 14-gauge angiocath and removes the needle, allowing pneumoperitoneum to push CO₂ out of the preperitoneal space, causing the space to collapse visibly. The fellow compresses the scrotum to expel CO₂, resulting in visible vibration of the peritoneum against the mesh.
